summary: Julia Evans recaps her experience at the Women in Machine Learning 2015 conference (part of NIPS), expressing enthusiasm for attending academic conferences outside her usual domain. She highlights three talks: Lillian Lee's research on how tweet phrasing affects retweets, Been Kim's work on interpretable ML and human-machine interaction, and Corinna Cortes' talk on web scraping for fact discovery at Google. A recurring theme is her appreciation for interpretable, debuggable ML and for speakers who are honest about how hard ML really is rather than pretending it's magic.
key_points: Academic conferences in unfamiliar fields can be more educational than industry conferences in your own field because everything is novel and practitioners have deep domain expertise.
ML applied to intrinsically hard tasks where even humans can't reach 100% accuracy is more interesting than ML on tasks humans find trivial.
Interpretable and debuggable ML systems that help humans perform better—rather than replacing them—represent an underappreciated area of research.
Honest discussion about how time-intensive and difficult building good ML models actually is counters the harmful narrative that ML is easy or magical.
Making technical talks accessible and interesting without sacrificing rigor is a deliberate and valuable presentation strategy.
money_quotes: I actually liked it a lot more than an industry conference because everything was so alien & unfamiliar to me and everyone knew way more about their field than me.
Doing ML on tasks that have something intrinsically hard about them – where you maybe can't ever get to 100% accuracy – is really interesting to me.
Convince people to be interested and that your work is obvious, not that you're smart and your work is difficult.
All I want is to help humans do better work & save them time so this made me super happy.
I think there's a lot of room in ML to enable people and not just replace them.
Instead of saying 'well at Google we do magic', the impression I got was 'dude this stuff is really hard and involves a lot of really boring HTML and tables'. BUT IN THE END WE WON.
IF YOU'RE NOT LOOKING AT YOUR DATA WHAT ARE YOU EVEN DOING.
I get really mad when people pretend that ML is easy or that if you're smart you can just get good results like magic.
key_insight: The most valuable machine learning work focuses on interpretability and helping humans do better work, not on replacing them or pretending the results come easily.
